questionText: Which two methods are the core of Java's Iterator interface?
correctAnswers: hasNext() and next()
explanation: java.util.Iterator centers on hasNext() (is there more?) and next() (return next element and advance).

questionText: Match each testing technique to its description.
matchingOptions: Verifies the code behaves correctly when given invalid input or when exceptions occur
Tests the 0 / 1 / many cases for collections and counts
Counts the number of linearly independent paths through a method (decision points + 1)
Divides inputs into classes that should be treated the same and tests one value per class
Designs tests at the edges of valid input ranges (min, max, just inside/outside)

correctAnswers.Boundary value analysis: Designs tests at the edges of valid input ranges (min, max, just inside/outside)
correctAnswers.Cardinality testing: Tests the 0 / 1 / many cases for collections and counts
correctAnswers.Cyclomatic complexity: Counts the number of linearly independent paths through a method (decision points + 1)
correctAnswers.Equivalence partitioning: Divides inputs into classes that should be treated the same and tests one value per class
correctAnswers.Error-condition testing: Verifies the code behaves correctly when given invalid input or when exceptions occur

explanation: Core black-box / white-box testing techniques.

questionText: Which design pattern does the following code implement?
correctAnswers: Iterator
explanation: An object exposing hasNext()/next() to traverse a collection without revealing its representation is the Iterator pattern.

questionText: Match each testing technique to its description.
matchingOptions: Re-runs existing tests to confirm that new changes did not break old behavior
Verifies the code behaves correctly when given invalid input or when exceptions occur
Counts the number of linearly independent paths through a method (decision points + 1)
Tests the 0 / 1 / many cases for collections and counts
Designs tests at the edges of valid input ranges (min, max, just inside/outside)

correctAnswers.Boundary value analysis: Designs tests at the edges of valid input ranges (min, max, just inside/outside)
correctAnswers.Cardinality testing: Tests the 0 / 1 / many cases for collections and counts
correctAnswers.Cyclomatic complexity: Counts the number of linearly independent paths through a method (decision points + 1)
correctAnswers.Error-condition testing: Verifies the code behaves correctly when given invalid input or when exceptions occur
correctAnswers.Regression testing: Re-runs existing tests to confirm that new changes did not break old behavior

explanation: Core black-box / white-box testing techniques.

questionText: Why is the Iterator pattern preferable to exposing a collection's internal list directly? Select 1 or more answers
options: It hides the internal representation of the collection
It lets multiple traversals proceed independently
It offers a uniform traversal interface across different collections
It guarantees the collection has a single instance
It converts the collection's interface to another one
correctAnswers: It hides the internal representation of the collection
It lets multiple traversals proceed independently
It offers a uniform traversal interface across different collections
explanation: Iterator encapsulates traversal, supports concurrent independent cursors, and unifies the interface; the last two options describe Singleton/Adapter.

Tests the 0 / 1 / many cases for collections and counts
Designs tests at the edges of valid input ranges (min, max, just inside/outside)

Re-runs existing tests to confirm that new changes did not break old behavior

correctAnswers.Boundary value analysis: Designs tests at the edges of valid input ranges (min, max, just inside/outside)
correctAnswers.Cardinality testing: Tests the 0 / 1 / many cases for collections and counts

correctAnswers.Error-condition testing: Verifies the code behaves correctly when given invalid input or when exceptions occur
correctAnswers.Regression testing: Re-runs existing tests to confirm that new changes did not break old behavior

explanation: Core black-box / white-box testing techniques.

questionText: A finance app has a fixed hierarchy of account types (Checking, Savings, Loan). You must keep adding new cross-cutting operations over them — interest report this quarter, tax export next quarter — without editing the account classes each time. Which single design pattern fits best?
correctAnswers: Visitor
explanation: Adding new operations over a stable set of element classes without modifying them is the Visitor pattern.

questionText: Match each FIRST unit-testing principle to its meaning.
matchingOptions: Tests should be written at the right time, ideally just before the production code
Tests must automatically report pass/fail without manual inspection
Tests must give the same result every time, in any environment
Tests must not depend on each other or on execution order
Tests should run quickly so they can be executed often

correctAnswers.Fast: Tests should run quickly so they can be executed often
correctAnswers.Independent: Tests must not depend on each other or on execution order
correctAnswers.Repeatable: Tests must give the same result every time, in any environment
correctAnswers.Self-validating: Tests must automatically report pass/fail without manual inspection
correctAnswers.Timely: Tests should be written at the right time, ideally just before the production code

explanation: FIRST = Fast, Independent, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely.

questionText: Which design pattern does the following code implement?
correctAnswers: Visitor
explanation: Elements exposing accept(visitor) that call back visitor.visit(this), with a visitor declaring a visit() overload per element type, is the Visitor pattern (double dispatch).

questionText: Match each FIRST unit-testing principle to its meaning.
matchingOptions: Tests must not depend on each other or on execution order
Tests must give the same result every time, in any environment
Tests must automatically report pass/fail without manual inspection

correctAnswers.Independent: Tests must not depend on each other or on execution order
correctAnswers.Repeatable: Tests must give the same result every time, in any environment
correctAnswers.Self-validating: Tests must automatically report pass/fail without manual inspection

explanation: FIRST = Fast, Independent, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely.

questionText: The Visitor pattern relies on 'double dispatch'. What does that mean here?
options: The operation invoked depends on both the element's runtime type and the visitor's type
Every method is called twice for safety
Two threads dispatch the call in parallel
The visitor clones itself before each visit
correctAnswers: The operation invoked depends on both the element's runtime type and the visitor's type
explanation: accept() selects the element type; the visit() overload selects the operation — two dispatches combine to pick the behavior.

questionText: Match each FIRST unit-testing principle to its meaning.
matchingOptions: Tests should run quickly so they can be executed often
Tests must automatically report pass/fail without manual inspection
Tests must give the same result every time, in any environment

correctAnswers.Fast: Tests should run quickly so they can be executed often
correctAnswers.Repeatable: Tests must give the same result every time, in any environment
correctAnswers.Self-validating: Tests must automatically report pass/fail without manual inspection

explanation: FIRST = Fast, Independent, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely.

questionText: Which design pattern (idiom) does the following code implement?
correctAnswers: Simple Factory
explanation: One method that switches on a parameter and returns a product instance is the Simple Factory idiom (not a formal GoF pattern).

questionText: Match each FIRST unit-testing principle to its meaning.
matchingOptions: Tests must give the same result every time, in any environment
Tests should run quickly so they can be executed often
Tests must not depend on each other or on execution order

correctAnswers.Fast: Tests should run quickly so they can be executed often
correctAnswers.Independent: Tests must not depend on each other or on execution order
correctAnswers.Repeatable: Tests must give the same result every time, in any environment

explanation: FIRST = Fast, Independent, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely.

questionText: Simple Factory vs Factory Method: which statement is TRUE?
options: Simple Factory centralises creation in one method/class; Factory Method defers the choice to subclasses
They are the same GoF pattern
Factory Method centralises creation in one method; Simple Factory uses subclasses
Simple Factory always returns a singleton
correctAnswers: Simple Factory centralises creation in one method/class; Factory Method defers the choice to subclasses
explanation: Simple Factory is one switch-like creator; Factory Method uses polymorphism so subclasses pick the concrete product.

questionText: Match each FIRST unit-testing principle to its meaning.
matchingOptions: Tests should run quickly so they can be executed often
Tests must give the same result every time, in any environment
Tests must not depend on each other or on execution order
Tests should be written at the right time, ideally just before the production code

correctAnswers.Fast: Tests should run quickly so they can be executed often
correctAnswers.Independent: Tests must not depend on each other or on execution order
correctAnswers.Repeatable: Tests must give the same result every time, in any environment
correctAnswers.Timely: Tests should be written at the right time, ideally just before the production code

explanation: FIRST = Fast, Independent, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely.

questionText: A document app needs one helper that, given a string extension ('pdf','docx','txt'), returns the right Document object, centralising this creation logic in a single place. Which creational approach is described (note: not a formal GoF pattern)?
correctAnswers: Simple Factory
explanation: Centralising parameterised object creation in a single method/class is the Simple Factory idiom.

questionText: Which are valid criticisms of the Simple Factory idiom? Select 1 or more answers
options: It tends to violate the Open/Closed Principle as new types are added
Its conditional chain grows with every new product
It can return null for unknown inputs
It cannot create objects at all
It forces every product to be a singleton
correctAnswers: It tends to violate the Open/Closed Principle as new types are added
Its conditional chain grows with every new product
It can return null for unknown inputs
explanation: Adding products means editing the factory (OCP), the if/switch grows, and unknown inputs may yield null. The last two options are false.

questionText: Which design pattern does the following code implement?
correctAnswers: Abstract Factory
explanation: A factory interface declaring multiple create methods for a family of related products, implemented per family, is Abstract Factory.

questionText: Factory Method vs Abstract Factory: which statement is TRUE?
options: Factory Method creates one product via subclassing; Abstract Factory creates whole families of related products
Abstract Factory creates exactly one product; Factory Method creates families
Abstract Factory is a behavioral pattern
correctAnswers: Factory Method creates one product via subclassing; Abstract Factory creates whole families of related products
explanation: Factory Method = one product through overriding; Abstract Factory = a set of related products behind one factory interface.

questionText: A cross-platform UI toolkit must produce matching sets of widgets — a Windows family (WinButton, WinScrollbar, WinMenu) and a macOS family (MacButton, MacScrollbar, MacMenu) — so that a whole consistent set is created together. Which single design pattern fits best?
correctAnswers: Abstract Factory
explanation: Creating coordinated families of related products behind one factory interface is the Abstract Factory pattern.

questionText: Which statements about Abstract Factory are TRUE? Select 1 or more answers
options: It creates families of related objects without naming their concrete classes
Each concrete factory produces one consistent product family
It guarantees a single global instance
It converts one interface into another
correctAnswers: It creates families of related objects without naming their concrete classes
Each concrete factory produces one consistent product family
It is a creational pattern
explanation: Abstract Factory is creational and builds consistent product families behind abstractions; it is not Singleton or Adapter.
